coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible carbon quantum dots for intelligent sensing in food safety applications: Opportunities and sustainability
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, A.
Ezati, P.
Kim, J.-T.
Rhim, J.-W.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely used in various industries due to their non-toxic, biocompatibility, antibacterial, and tunable photoluminescence properties. In particular, interest in developing smart/intelligent sensing systems using CQDs is increasing in the food industry. The integration of CQDs-sensing platforms with food packaging could provide a means to allow real-time monitoring of the quality change of packaged foods. A novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, CQD, is considered a promising candidate for developing novel intelligent nanosensors for food quality control. Therefore, it is noteworthy to analyze new information on the proficiency of CQD-based sensing systems in developing sustainable, innovative packaging materials. Although CQDs have been used for several potential applications, the focus of the current review is to summarize recent research and developments on CQD-based sensors that can be used in the food industry. This review also outlines the potential challenges and prospects of modern CQDs and real-time intelligent sensing of food quality changes.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible carbon quantum dots for intelligent sensing in food safety applications: Opportunities and sustainability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely used in various industries due to their non-toxic, biocompatibility, antibacterial, and tunable photoluminescence properties. In particular, interest in developing smart/intelligent sensing systems using CQDs is increasing in the food industry. The integration of CQDs-sensing platforms with food packaging could provide a means to allow real-time monitoring of the quality change of packaged foods. A novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, CQD, is considered a promising candidate for developing novel intelligent nanosensors for food quality control. Therefore, it is noteworthy to analyze new information on the proficiency of CQD-based sensing systems in developing sustainable, innovative packaging materials. Although CQDs have been used for several potential applications, the focus of the current review is to summarize recent research and developments on CQD-based sensors that can be used in the food industry. This review also outlines the potential challenges and prospects of modern CQDs and real-time intelligent sensing of food quality changes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Preventing food contamination to ensure food safety and maintain food quality is of paramount importance to both producers and consumers [1]. Consumers are increasingly concerned about food safety as billions worldwide are at risk of consuming unsafe food [2]. In addition to food poisoning, microorganisms that can contaminate food, hazards in food processing, heavy metals in water sources, incidental toxins or products in food, and numerous harmful pesticide residues in agriculture can also cause food spoilage and pose a threat to food safety [3]. Dietary dissatisfaction resulting from inadequate food safety has serious implications for health and economic organization in less developed countries. Therefore, food safety today is more critical than ever and has become a top concern worldwide. Food safety investigation and detection is a vital technology path to ensure food safety. Conventional analytical detection techniques such as liquid-mass spectrometry, gas-mass spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, and gas chromatography have high accuracy and sensitivity. Still, they have some serious drawbacks, such as relatively long time-consuming, complex operation, expensive equipment, the need for technical and professional personnel, which cannot encounter the requirements for effective and real-time monitoring of the entire chain of food production, processing, storage, and supply [4,5]. Therefore, to secure food safety, a fast, sensitive, and accurate detection system is urgently needed to expand food safety detection technology. Accordingly, smart/intelligent sensing technology that detects changes in food quality or microbial contamination in real-time during food storage or distribution is emerging [6]. For example, it has been reported that developing portable systems for use with food packaging has led to detecting quality changes and spoilage in packaged food and enabled communication with users [7]. These technologies help to improve the preservation, storage, transportation, and handling of food, prevent leakage of substances in food into the environment and significantly increase safety from potential external contaminants [7,8].

Apart from this, numerous new detection methods and technologies have also been developed to improve food quality and safety monitoring, including temperature and time freshness indicators, contamination and pathogen biosensors, and microbial spoilage biosensors for packaged foods, providing satisfactory results [9]. However, progress in this field is still in its infancy despite much research and development. Much research is needed to achieve the end goal of having generic food packaging capabilities that can monitor the safety and quality of food at a commercial level.

With the rapid advances in nanoscience, nanotechnology provides a simple and fast detection method for food safety measurements, which may offer the opportunity to overcome food safety challenges through the food supply chain [10]. Compared with conventional detection technology, nanomaterials-based methods have the advantages of target recognition and greatly improved sensitivity, selectivity, and signal information [11]. A variety of efficient nanomaterials, such as carbon-based materials [12], semiconductors [13], and metallic nanoparticles [14], have been designed to monitor quality changes in packaged foods. Above all, nanomaterials combined with fluorescence-based detection technology have the advantages of speed, sensitivity, simplicity, low cost, and portability and have gradually become a potential route for food safety decisions. However, unique fluorescent nanomaterials such as quantum dots (QDs) have low biocompatibility and high toxicity, while heavy metal nanoclusters are expensive and have low stability [15]. These inadequacies limit their practical application in food safety analysis.

In the past decade, metal-free carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received considerable attention due to their unique optical properties, water solubility, promising biocompatibility, high stability, and functional properties without cytotoxicity [16–18]. CQDs can be easily and economically prepared using several chemicals and biomass as precursors [19]. It has also been shown that several functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and epoxy, on the surface of CQDs impart water solubility and make them suitable for further modification with various moieties [20]. According to published reports, CQDs have been extensively developed for photoelectric conversion, bio-imaging system biosensing, and photocatalytic investigations thanks to their excellent fluorescence properties [21–25]. Various food contaminants, including veterinary drugs, bacteria, pesticides, prohibited additives, advanced glycosylation end products, and biogenic amines, can be detected using CQD probes, indicating broad application in food analysis [15,17,25]. Therefore, as an advantageous replacement for conventional QDs, CQDs have found more promising applications in the trace-level detection of food contaminants and other analytes. Fig. 1 shows the potential uses of CQDs in food technology.

The main objective of the current review is to summarize the basic concepts regarding the use of CQDs for the intelligent/smart detection of contaminants and monitoring of quality changes in food and how CQDs can be used to make more accurate recall decisions. The focus was on discussing CQDs' synthetic pathways and properties and examining how the integration of CQDs could advance food packaging to report food safety, quality, or possible perishability of food. This review also outlined the potential of CQD-based sensors for detecting food additives and various desired analytes and finally discussed the challenges and future directions for CQDs in food technology.

Carbon nanomaterials are used as promising and advanced functional materials in multiple fields, such as biotechnology and energy resources [26]. Since zero-dimensional fullerenes were first investigated in the 1980s, three-dimensional diamonds, two-dimensional graphene, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphite have emerged as promising nanomaterials [27]. Photoluminescent CQDs were first discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. during single-walled carbon nanotube purification using the arc-discharge method [28]. As promising candidates for zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles (NPs), CQDs with a typical size of less than 10 nm are quasi-spherical NPs. They consisted of a carbon core and a surface shell with functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups [27,29]. CQDs can be mainly classified into three types depending on the carbon precursor/core: carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and polymer dots (PDs) [30]. PDs are usually derived from monomeric or linear polymers, aggregated or crosslinked polymeric nanoparticles. CNDs are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles without a discontinuous crystalline matrix. These CQDs consist of sp2/sp3 hybridized amorphous carbon atoms and embedded sp2 hybridized nanocrystalline clusters [31]. CQDs are highly interesting compared to other carbon nanomaterials due to their advanced physicochemical properties, such as easy functionalization, nontoxicity, biocompatibility, excellent fluorescent intensity, abundant carbon source, water-solubility, photoinduced redox properties, excellent electron-transfer efficiency, and rich edge defects [19]. The outstanding effects of molecular states, surface defects, and quantum confinement provide CQDs with remarkable fluorescence intensity [32]. In addition, CQDs doped with heteroatoms such as S, B, P, F, and N can optimize the electronic structure to lower the sensing activity significantly [33]. These advantages make CQDs enormous potential as promising nanomaterials for food safety and quality applications.

Various approaches have been successfully developed and synthesized high-efficiency CQDs with consistent properties. For the effective synthesis of high-quality CQDs based on carbon sources, two methods are commonly used: “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods (Fig. 2 ) [34]. CQDs manufactured with a top-down approach involve the decomposition of carbonaceous materials (e.g., graphite powder, graphene, graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes, coal, etc.) through chemical, physical or electrochemical methods [35]. Breaking technology has been extended to electrochemical oxidation under high potential, acid etching, and laser ablation [30]. Although top-down approaches can generate effective CQDs, the harsh condition requirements for these methods sometimes create difficulties in controlling responses.

On the other hand, the bottom-up approach lies in chemical synthesis and employs pyrolysis and a few chemical reactions to facilitate the carbonization of small organic molecules, artificial compounds, or biomass materials [35]. Hydrothermal/solvothermal, ultrasonic oscillation, microwave irradiation, and heat treatment are typically bottom-up approaches. In these methods, it is essential to use lattice environments such as alkali or acid-assisted oxidation reactions, chemical carbonization, heat treatment, and combustion to initiate the process [35]. Compared with top-down, bottom-up is simpler, cheaper, and more extensively applied. The most common CQD synthesis methods and their advantages and disadvantages are summarized in Fig. 2.

Due to the wide range of carbon resources and synthetic techniques, CQDs possess various physical and chemical properties. However, the fundamental features of different CQDs are high stability, tunable fluorescence, environmental friendliness, nontoxicity, and water solubility [36,37]. In this section, the main characteristic features of CQDs are summarized in detail.

CQDs exhibit apparent light absorption in the UV region (230–320 nm), with tails extending into the visible region due to edge-linked chemical groups and π-conjugated electrons in the sp2 atomic structure. A broad peak appears around ∼230 nm due to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic C=C bond (indicating that CQDs possess photon-harvesting ability). In contrast, the shouldering peak around ∼300 nm is caused by the n-π∗ transition of C=O bonds or other related groups. Depending on the position of the absorption band, detailed formation mechanisms and properties can be identified [38]. In addition, the morphology, size of the π-conjugates, structure, or composition of the hybridized derivatives, such as surface passivation and functional groups, can also stimulate the absorption of CQDs. The bandgap fluorescence in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs shows an apparent exciton absorption band due to the quantum confinement effect [19].

There are also studies suggesting that various parameters can control the adsorption properties of CQDs. The bathochromic exciton absorption band shift is constant as the size of the CQDs increases progressively. Furthermore, different functional groups incorporated during CQD synthesis (C=N, O–C=O, C=O, and C–OH) can generate surface states with energy levels between π and π∗ states [39,40] and promote absorption characteristics because of the electron transitions. In general, the characteristics of CQDs are determined by molecular states or surface defects, and they emit weak light only in the UV region [41]. However, these chromogenic shifts are thought to occur due to heteroatom doping or surface modification. For example, it has been reported that S, N codoped CQDs exhibited two absorption peaks at about 550 and 595 nm due to π-π∗ and n-π∗ of C=S and S=O, respectively [42].

Since the most useful property of CQDs is their photoluminescence (PL) properties, it is essential to understand the basics of the PL properties of CQDs. However, the exact mechanism of PL is not yet clear. Still, it can be obtained from defect-state and intrinsic state emission [43], which may be due to quantum confinement effects (QCE), emission traps, oxygen-containing groups, aromatic structures, edge defects, and free zigzag sites [44]. The PL of CQDs is affected by the size, surface functionalization, heteroatom doping, solvent polarity, surface oxidation, pH, and excitation wavelength [19]. Zhi et al. prepared malic acid carbon dots (MACDs) using ethylenediamine as a cross-linker and self-passivating agent using a microwave-assisted approach [45]. The MACDs were partitioned into fractions with different particle sizes through C-18 reversed-phase silica gel column chromatography. This route assisted the analyses of the proposed CQDs' optical properties, particularly bandgap energies. An extinction shoulder at 470 nm was noticed, summarizing the bathochromic shift that signified the bandgap contraction, measured through optical bandgap energy (Eg). The measured Eg values were 2.97, 2.91, and 2.21 eV for the particle sizes of 6.2 ± 2.0, 9.2 ± 1.7, and 15.6 ± 6.0 nm, respectively, which determine the effect of the particle size distribution on the bandgap energy of the fraction. Further parameters impacts through the particle size distribution of 6.2 ± 2.0, 9.2 ± 1.7, and 15.6 ± 6.0 nm were average fluorescence lifetime of 6.48 ± 0.10, 7.14 ± 0.63, 4.25 ± 0.09 ns, the quantum yield of 30, 24, 27.6%, and Stokes shift of 16,822, 4,959, 2,644.3 cm−1, respectively [45]. They found that the particle size of the MACDs impacts the PL characteristics and bandgap energy.

In most cases, CQDs have excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics, making CQDs a potential candidate for analyte detection [46]. This phenomenon occurs due to the interaction between the analyte and the CQD, either by decreasing the fluorescence by quenching or increasing the fluorescence via suppressing the quenching effect. However, little attention has been paid to the mechanism of fluorescence emission. The quenching mechanism can be subdivided into the internal filter effect, photoinduced electron transfer, static, dynamic, and energy transfer [46]. In static quenching, the interaction between the quencher and the CQD results in a non-fluorescent ground state molecule, which after absorbing light, returns to the ground state immediately without emitting a photon [47]. In dynamic quenching, collisions between CQDs and quenchers induce excited CQDs back to the ground state using charge transfer or energy transfer mechanisms [47]. Energy transfer is classified into surface energy transfer, facilitation resonance energy, and Dexter energy transfer. The photon energy of the donor (the first fluorophore) is required through the acceptor (the second fluorophore). It facilitates the transfer of the emitted resonance energy through the second fluorophore [46]. Facilitated resonance energy transfer occurs between the quencher in the ground state and the CQD in the excited state when the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps the emission spectrum of the CQD. Liang et al. reported a detailed investigation into the fluorescence quenching mechanism using chitosan-based CQDs as a model [48]. They summarized the quenching behavior of CQDs by adopting a nitroaromatic series with different ring substituents. The results show that, despite electron transfer, facilitated resonance energy transfer is the primary mechanism of CQD quenching. Other than this, no additional mechanisms are known to explain the luminescence of CQDs.

Besides this, another factor affecting the fluorescence of CQDs is called the internal filter effect (IFE). IFE, sometimes referred to as apparent quenching, is not due to the quenching process but rather because the absorption of the emitted radiation or excitation beam is attenuated by the excessive concentration of CQDs or quenching agents in the solution [49]. Therefore, IFE usually occurs when the absorption spectrum of the quencher in the detection system overlaps with the emission or excitation spectrum of the CQDs. This effect also leads to a decrease in strength (but not decay time), but this effect should not be called “quenching”. The IFE also arises if distances between re-absorber and emitted surpass 10 nm. As the phenomenon of IFE does not belong to the dynamic or static quenching process, the absorption peaks of the CQDs would not alter; it also specifies that there is no new component to form. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs is not changed. Moreover, the Parker equation can further demonstrate the IFE process [49,50].

Foodborne bacteria are the most common microbes responsible for food contamination and waste [51]. Therefore, to avoid food spoilage due to bacterial contamination, antimicrobial agents could be added to the packaging system to prevent the growth of microbes by extending the lag period and decreasing the live counts of microorganisms by reducing the growth rate. The antimicrobial agent is incorporated either into the packaging material or directly into food products, which is released over a period to preserve the food's quality and safety, leading to its extended shelf life [52]. In this regard, CQDs exert excellent antibacterial efficacy against a broad spectrum of microbial strains owing to their exclusive physicochemical properties, such as structural defects, small size, surface charge, and functional groups [53]. In the past decade, CQDs have generated tremendous interest in food safety and biomedical applications owing to excellent antimicrobial efficacy. Numerous investigations demonstrated that CQDs with a better dispersion and smaller diameter would promote more efficient antimicrobial function against a wide range of microorganisms, viruses, bacteria, and fungi. The magnitude and nature of the charge on the surface of CQDs can critically impact their interaction with bacterial cell membranes [53]. For example, Bing et al. synthesized neutral, positively, and negatively charged CQDs through glucose, spermine, and candle soot [54]. They found that only positively charged CQDs possessed the highest bacterial apoptosis; negatively charged showed a weaker effect, while neutral CQDs did not affect bacterial growth. Thus, it can be suggested that the electrical properties of CQDs play a critical part in antibacterial activity. Anionic and neutral CQDs are repelled by bacterial cells, while cationic CQDs attract negative charges in bacterial cells.

In addition to the physicochemical characteristics of CQDs, their size, shape, and surface functional groups can impact antibacterial efficacy. In another report, Kousheh et al. reported Lactobacillus acidophilus-derived CQDs and their antibacterial action against gram-negative and gram-positive bacterial strains [55]. The as-prepared CQDs possessed higher antibacterial potential against both E. coli and L. monocytogenes because of the electrostatic repulsive forces between the CQDs and bacteria. Similar to surface charge modification, surface functionalization CQDs with different heteroatoms and metals can also significantly enhance the antibacterial function. For instance, Travlou et al. synthesized nitrogen and sulfur-doped CQDs (N-CDs and S-CDs) for antibacterial analyses [56]. For N-CDs, amines and amides exhibited a critically significant part in improving bactericidal efficacy. The S-CDs attained a negatively charged surface and displayed size-dependent antibacterial activity. Likewise, zinc and nitrogen codoped CQDs (Zn, N-CDs) showed brilliant bacteriostatic effects against E. coli [57].

Apart from this, Lin et al. investigated the antimicrobial action via adhesion between CQDs and bacterial cells and overwhelmed electrostatic repulsion through the van der Waals forces of attraction [58]. In addition, several reports also revealed that variations in the cell wall structure combined with dopants and doping materials could suggestively influence the antimicrobial efficacy of CQDs against, unlike microbial cells. For example, Ezati et al. prepared hydrothermally assisted heteroatom (boron, sulfur, and nitrogen atoms) doped CQDs using glucose as the main carbon precursor and compared their antifungal and antibacterial efficacies against a wide range of microorganisms [53]. The experimental outcomes suggested that the heteroatom-doped CQDs displayed stronger antimicrobial potential because of the presence of specific functional groups (sulfonic and amides functional groups) on the surface of CQDs. Predominantly, N-doped CQD exhibited the highest antimicrobial potential against bacteria (E. coli, L. monocytogenes), mold (P. citrinum, F. solani, A. fumigatus), and yeast (R. rubra, C. albicans) strains. This investigation also revealed that the doping of CQDs with heteroatoms improves the interaction interactions of CQDs with microbial cells because of the generation of additional reactive oxygen species (ROS), which consequently exhibited higher antimicrobial potential. Further, Zhao et al. prepared N-CQDs and demonstrated their antibacterial actions against a wide range of microorganisms such as S. aureus (ATCC6538), S. aureus (ATCC43300), S. epidermidis, and MRSA (Fig. 3 a,b) [59]. The experimental outcomes of disk diffusion assays revealed strong antibacterial efficacy of N-CQDs against Staphylococcus strains. They suggested the specific antimicrobial efficacy because the N-CQDs combined with negatively charged bacterial strain and then anchored to the specific sites on the Staphylococcus' surface to cause the rupture and apoptosis of bacteria cells. Yang et al. evaluated the antibacterial activity of quaternized CQDs (CDs-C12) in vivo, the mice infected with S. aureus were established as a model (Fig. 3c–e). They found that mice treated with CDs-C12 resulted in a bacterial burden of 2.8 × 106 CFU/g exhibited a significant decrease in the bacterial number (with a bacterial killing efficiency of 98.7%) than the control. The proposed principle of CDs-C12 for killing and selective recognition of Gram-positive bacterial strains is illustrated in Fig. 3f [60]. As shown in Fig. 3g, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images demonstrated the damage of bacterial membranes (E. coli and S. aureus) due to the exposure of fluorescent CQDs (F-CQD) and LED. At a concentration of 512 μg/mL FCDs, bacterial cells exhibited signs of envelope stress, evident in increased membrane blebbing and lysis [61]. Overall, several properties of CQDs on bacterial have been identified as a probable antibacterial efficacy pathway; thus, the coupled action of diverse mechanisms regarding the bacterial apoptosis of CQDs was represented as followings: (i) Production of ROS (HO2 −, OH− and O2 −) via CQD. The produced free radical groups are available to interact with electron-hole pairs, which are anticipated to generate ˙OH, corresponding to cell wall degradation, leading to cell machinery failure and ultimate death. (ii) Interruption of metabolic progressions caused via interaction of CQD with target molecules on the bacterial cell wall. (iii) CQD's interaction with DNA replication within the microbial cell results in cell death [56,62,63].

Oxidation of lipids in food like fish oils, vegetables, nuts, seafood, and meat is a significant cause of their spoilage [64]. Lipid oxidation in food products results in the development of off-flavors, typical of rancidity, rendering the product inappropriate for consumers. Therefore, to ensure food safety, a few strategies have been adapted, such as designing an appropriate packaging technology comprising suitable antioxidant agents and directly adding antioxidants to foods. The release of antioxidant agents into the food scavenges or neutralizes the unfavorable compounds such as oxygen, metal ions, or radical oxidative species generated during food storage. Antioxidants or scavengers are components that interact with, trap, or modify substances that are involved in any oxidation process [64]. Therefore, an appropriate selection of the antioxidant substance to be incorporated in the packaging material is crucial. For this purpose, the antioxidant efficacy of CQD could be significant for the food packaging sector. Recent investigations have clarified CQDs' free radical scavenging ability at the cellular level with projected mechanisms. For instance, Zhang et al. synthesized nitrogen and sulfur-codoped florescent CQDs (N, S-codoped CND), and dose-dependent antioxidant efficacy was carried out towards lucigenin chemiluminescence (lucigenin-CL) and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH•) assay through electrochemistry measurements and UV–vis absorption [65]. The antioxidant activity against DPPH• was calculated to be 38.9%, 52.2%, 65.1%, 76.7%, and 87.4% for 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.10 mg/mL concentrations of N, S-codoped CND, respectively (Fig. 4 a and b). Whereas the scavenging efficacy of lucigenin-CL was quenched by about 59% at 0.10 mg/mL of N, S-codoped CND (Fig. 4c and d). They suggested that N, S-codoped CND exhibited antioxidant efficacy, as the lucigenin-CL assay perceives superoxide anion radicals, i.e., ROS generated via xanthine/xanthine oxidase reaction. Markovic et al. synthesized fluorine (F) and chlorine (Cl) doped CQDs using urea and citric acid as the primary carbon source. The scavenging efficacy determined by the DPPH• essay demonstrated that both F-CQDs and Cl-CQDs possessed excellent antioxidant activity (Fig. 4e–h) [66]. In another report, Ezati et al. confirmed the antioxidant activity of heteroatom (B, S, and N) doped CQDs derived from glucose as the primary carbon source. The free radical scavenging efficacy of the B, S, and N-CQDs was determined using ABTS and DPPH assays, and the results were compared with the most common antioxidant, ascorbic acid [53]. The results revealed that undoped CQDs displayed 100% free radical scavenging efficacy against DPPH and ABTS at 50 μg/mL concentrations. In contrast, the free radical scavenging efficacy of ascorbic acid at the same concentration was found to be 53% and 80%, respectively.

On the other hand, nitrogen-doped CQDs displayed 30% and 65% free radical scavenging efficacy against DPPH and ABTS at a much lower concentration of 12 μg/mL, while ascorbic acid showed only ∼20% scavenging ability for both DPPH and ABTS. The obtained results concluded that the significant enhancement in the antioxidant activity was due to amide groups arising from nitrogen doping, hydroxyl groups, and stable dispersion in the aqueous medium. The formation of ROS could be the main reason for the antioxidant activity of CQDs. Such free radicals can be quenched and reduced by hydrogen donation, electron transfer, and adduct formation from the peroxynitrite, nitric oxide, hydroxyl, and superoxide anion of the CQDs via intracellular and extracellular interactions [65,67,68]. As a result, it could be proposed that CQD possesses high potential in numerous applications, such as food packaging and biological systems, where free radical scavenging efficacy is crucial.

Carbon materials are investigated to be eco-friendly and used in routine life with negligible cytotoxicity. Nevertheless, the cytotoxic impact of carbon nanomaterials on humans should be assessed, as they may enter the human body through injection, skin, gastrointestinal digestion, absorption, respiration, etc. [69]. Therefore, prior consideration, it is necessary to determine the cytotoxicity of CQDs to ensure safety in food packaging or biomedical applications. CQDs that can be derived from non-toxic raw materials through eco-friendly methods comprise not only antioxidant and antibacterial potential but also possess brilliant biocompatibility. The efficient biocompatibility of CQDs makes them enables food packaging, biolabeling, antibacterial drug delivery, and cell imaging applications [70,71]. The previous reports described the cytotoxic effect of food-derived CQDs in food items, including coffee, lamb, barbecue, hamburgers, roast duck, salmon, honey, cola, pizza, bread, and beer, etc. [72]. However, in vivo and in vitro toxicity analyses of CQD are vital in the food sector from a promising application outlook. Up to now, the cytotoxicity of CQDs has been investigated several times at the cellular level. For instance, Cailotto et al. (2018) reported a CellTiter-Glo® Luminescence assay to examine the cytotoxicity of ascorbic acid, fructose, and glucose CQD towards HeLa cells for 96 h with the Infinite 200 PRO instrument (Tecan) [73]. They found that even at glucose-based CQD concentration of 1000 μg/mL, the cells retained high viability, whereas ascorbic acid-based CQD displayed good biocompatibility at low concentrations (<250 μg/mL). Tan et al. demonstrated that the grilled fish-derived CQDs showed high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity toward mouse osteoblast cells (MC3T3-E1), even at a high concentration of 20 mg/mL [74]. Yousaf et al. reported the competence of fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQDs) for determining the in vitro inhibitory effect on aggregation and cytotoxicity of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) [75]. Due to the presence of hydrophobic groups and a high charge density, FGQDs could bind with the hIAPP monomer and inhibit the conformational transition by prolonging the aggregation time of hIAPP, thereby preventing the formation of mature fibrils. Mangifera indica (mGQDs) derived red-fluorescent GQDs with a size of 2–8 nm were investigated to be highly biocompatible with the mouse fibroblast cell line L929 even at high concentration (0.1 mg/mL) 24 h post-treatment [76]. Li et al. reported that the hemolysis of human RBCs was insignificant in the existence of spermidine-capped fluorescent carbon quantum dots (Spd-CQDs), even when the Spd-CQDs concentration was 100-fold higher than the MIC for bacteria [77]. In another report, Jian et al. reported that the treatment of CQD (derived from direct pyrolysis of spermidine (CQDspds)) towards bacterial keratitis possessed a negligible cytotoxic effect against rabbit corneal keratocytes (RCK cells), even though a structural interaction was noticed between RCK cells and CQDspds [78]. They also validated that CQDspds were harmless to human RBCs and did not cause oxidative or genotoxic damage and hemagglutination at 100 mg/mL concentrations. Zhao et al. determine the in vivo and in vitro toxicity of the N-CQDs. For in vitro analysis the viability of mammalian cells such as PANC-1 and HeLa exposed to variable different N-CQD concentrations using MTT assays for 24 h [59]. At a 0.128 mg/mL concentration of N-CQD <80%, cell viability was observed, whereas toxicity increases depending on the concentration in the two cell lines (Fig. 5 a). The in vivo toxicity investigations were performed on mice by exposing them to the concentration of 5 mg/mL of N-CQDs for 7 days and observing the morphology of five main organs, such as the kidney, heart, liver, spleen, and lung, using an optical microscope (Fig. 5b). The obtained results displayed no pathological alteration in any tissue that interacted with N-CQD, which was clarified through N-CQD conversion into non-toxic components because of digestion. The CQDs’ interaction with biological structures is a rather multifaceted phenomenon. Results depend on the assay method used for the cytotoxicity assay and the cell system being investigated. Furthermore, this interaction depends on other parameters such as surface charge, functional groups, surface coating, shape, size, etc. Nevertheless, in most of the discussion, cytotoxicity investigations of CQDs revealed that concentrations in the range of 10–100 μg/mL were responsible for a slight loss in cell viability (∼10–20%) and suggested to be safe for food processing and biomedical applications.

During the synthesis, processing, employment, disposal, and recovery progressions, CQDs will unavoidably be discharged into the environments. The hydrophilic nature of CQDs impacts the natural ecosystem, particularly living organisms, foods, and aquatic systems. Therefore, the probable impact of CQDs on the natural world cannot be overlooked. Recent studies have confirmed that CQDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility by analyzing cytotoxic effects based on specific cell bacteria, several terrestrial organisms such as mice and rats, and aquatic zebrafish [79–82]. Post ingestion to the organism, CQDs did not cause substantial pathological alterations. They can be released from the body of organisms (e.g., such as mice, rats, and zebrafish through normal metabolic pathways. On the other hand, numerous reports have revealed that CQDs positively impact plants, including their growth and resistance to biological and abiotic stress at the appropriate application concentration [83–86]. The optimal dose of CQDs can endorse the growth of plants via promoting nitrogen fixation by Azotobacter, nutrient assimilation, and accelerating photosynthesis. Determining the levels of ecological impacts and biological toxicity of CQDs is of prodigious significance during the development phases for the accurate understanding and valuation of the potential environmental risks of its utilization. Moreover, the influences of CQDs on ecosystems and biological inhabitants are rarely analyzed, which should be assessed prudently in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and disadvantages of CQDs for food sensing applications
ce:section.ce:para: A literature review indicates that analyzing food contaminants presents the most significant challenge for analytical scientists because of their heterogeneity. Natural substances such as vitamins, proteins, and other essential organic compounds are often mistakenly recognized as food components due to their variable density, functional groups, size, and type while using food quality analysis. Determining the desired analyte to ensure food safety requires a variety of sophisticated analytical instruments. Sophisticated methods such as gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and enzyme immunoassay have been used to detect food components and risk factors. Due to their outstanding sensitivity and accuracy, these techniques have been widely used to detect numerous analytes to ensure food safety. However, most of these techniques have been seldom used for food samples due to their drawbacks of laborious and time-consuming sample preparation, costly equipment, and skilled operators [87].

Additionally, manually identifying food degradation due to other interference issues is a tedious and time-consuming analysis step. Manual staining can slip the identification of hard-to-distinguish elements of food spoilage. To overcome these drawbacks, advanced analytical techniques supported by safe, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly materials are essential to obtain reliable results. Complex food matrices also anticipate a susceptible and selective measurement to enable on-site, real-time, rapid food safety detection. Consumers will undoubtedly be much more educated about food authenticity, quality, and traceability [88]. In almost every industry, nanotechnology is believed to have enormous potential to create new products, and many applications are already available on the global market. The safety of nanoparticles remains questionable but should be used to generate risk assessments for use in the food industry. Nanotechnology also promises fascinating perspectives for the food industry along the entire food chain [89]. As nanotechnology is a newly developed field, it offers the possibility of overcoming the difficulties of food detection. Nanomaterial-based biosensors exhibit more accurate target recognition, higher selectivity and sensitivity, improved signal readout, and faster assay times than conventional chromatography techniques [87].

Nevertheless, the challenge of ensuring food safety can be addressed by developing hybrid nanomaterials such as CQDs and combining them with appropriate existing devices. CQDs can play a variety of roles in a variety of nanobiosensing-based methods. Due to their relatively large surface area and porous structure, they can act as carriers or enhancers. They may also act as catalysts based on high peroxidase activity, reporters that can be used as electrochemical, colorimetric, fluorescent, or other types of signal molecules, quenchers based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE), or separators in the form of magnetic nanoparticles [90,91].

Sensing technologies based on changes in fluorescence are made possible by changes in CQD synthesis. There are many sensors available for sensing purposes. More and more articles are focusing on CQD probes for food safety and various food contaminants (e.g., bacteria, veterinary drugs, pesticides, and banned additives), demonstrating the widespread use of CDs in food analysis [5]. The majority of CQDs-based sensors rely on single emission intensity measurements of photoluminescence, which can be influenced by variables such as ambient influences in complex samples, the drift of the light source or detector, and the concentration of the probe in the detecting system.

However, the complex food matrices and countless tiny chemicals provide the sensing platform of the CQDs with remarkable challenges. It is challenging to create an ideal aqueous solution free of interferences to detect food quality and safety through pretreatment of the original food substrate, even though most elemental analyses are carried out in aqueous solutions with few interferences [92]. The interferences in the sensing system may then interact with the CQDs sensors, changing the fluorescence signals and lowering the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensors. Interactions between the ligands of CQDs and target analytes or interfering molecules mainly control the detection signal from changes in fluorescence intensity. Additionally, the complex food matrix contains a variety of active substances that may attack the ligands of a rationally designed CQDs sensor, resulting in a different surface state and muddled fluorescence signals [93]. Therefore, numerous efforts have been made to improve the sensors' sensitivity, selectivity, and anti-interference ability through the expert identification of a particular ligand, to circumvent the shortcomings of the CQDs sensing platform for food safety. Nevertheless, since CQDs have a similar morphology and basic configuration, many situations affect the detection signal. Ratiometric fluorescence sensing has recently been recognized as an effective method for CQD-based sensors to accurately address the need to detect target analytes [66,93].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CQDs in food technology
ce:section.ce:para: As a new class of multifunctional nanomaterials, CQDs exhibit attractive properties such as tunable bandgap, good water solubility, high photostability, strong photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [94]. These advantages allow CQDs to act as fluorescent probes to detect unsafe additives and substances such as tartrazine mycotoxins, food-borne pathogens, toxic heavy metals, and other toxic compounds [94]. CQDs have been widely used as fluorescent candidates to function as fluorescent probes in food detection applications. CQDs can interact with various compounds due to their large surface functional groups, altering their optical properties. Table 1 lists the sensing applications of chemical compound-derived CQD-based sensors in the food sector, including detecting restricted or prohibited additives, nutritional substances, microbial toxins, pathogenic microorganisms, and pesticides. Table 2 summarizes the biomass-derived CQDs-based sensing probes in food and real sample applications. Recently, CQDs have aroused extraordinary interest in exploiting the selective and sensitive detectability of small molecules [94]. Fluorescent CQDs, synthesized or surface-functionalized with specific organic compounds or combined with analyte sensing elements, can be used to determine desired analytes. Especially in food safety applications, they can detect or sense food additives, heavy metals, pathogens, microbial toxins, pesticides, and changes in pH values [67].

CQDs' outstanding photostability and photoluminescence properties have been used to detect target molecules [128]. Intentionally consuming prohibited or restricted food additives and nutritional substances can lead to diseases like cancer and serious human health risks [129]. It has already been reported that the successful application of CQDs in detecting food additives and nutrients such as ascorbic acid, tannic acid, carmine, tartrazine, nitrite, glutathione, sunset yellow, etc., poses a significant risk to human health. Interactions between CQDs and target analytes were site-specific, forming covalent or coordination bonds [130].

For instance, Chatzimitakos et al. used citrus lemon CQDs to determine tartrazine concentrations with synthetic material in lemon peels in the food additive aspect [115]. As reported, excessive consumption of tartrazine poses significant risks to human health [131]. The effectiveness of the CQD sensing method was confirmed by comparing it with actual food analysis results in ice cream, juice, and energy drinks. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was decreased by adding 80.0 μM of tartrazine through a quenching mechanism. The PL spectra of CQDs showed a linear relationship with increasing tartrazine concentrations in 0.6–23.5 μM [115]. Tartrazine in 0.25–32.50 mM was also successfully detected by fluorescence quenching of CQDs in food samples [102]. In another study, CQDs with 11% quantum yield were constructed using dried lemon peel by a hydrothermal method at 200 °C. The synthesized CQDs possessed an 11% quantum yield and displayed high emission under the wavelength of 505 nm. At 508 nm wavelength, carmine could selectively quench the fluorescence of CQDs with maximum absorption. The developed CQDs were used as fluorescence probes for the sensitive determination of carmine in drinks, with a detection limit of 0.16 mg/L [116]. Zhang et al. reported hydrothermally assisted nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) using prawn shells as carbon sources and used as fluorophores for the sensitive and selective detection of NO2 − in water. The experimental outcomes suggested that detecting NO2− due to the direct fluorescent quenching at N-CNDs fluorophores can attain a detection limit of 1.0 μM with a linear detection range of up to 1.0 mM. The principle of fluorescent quenching is the presence of N-functional groups that impart a positive surface charge to the CQDs, leading to the attraction of anion charges (NO−) in water [117].

Dai et al. used a fluorescence resonance energy transfer system with gold nanoparticles as energy acceptors and CQDs as energy donors to detect melamine in food [100]. Under optimal conditions, they obtained a detection limit of 36 nM for melamine and a liner range of 50–500 nM, depending on the PL intensity of the CQDs. These results confirmed the system's feasibility by quantifying melamine in milk samples. As a toxic pesticide, residual glyphosate poses a severe environmental problem. Wang et al. prepared glyphosate antibody-CQDs to detect glyphosate in situ and demonstrated a detection limit of 8 ng/mL for glyphosate [114]. The PL spectra of the developed CQDs showed a linear relationship when the glyphosate concentration increased in the range of 0.01–80 μg/mL. As CQD was applied to detect glyphosate in tea, satisfactory recovery rates (87.4–103.7%) were achieved [114]. Li et al. used N and S co-doped CQDs for trace-level analysis of ultrasensitive pesticides. They built a simple, sensitive, and efficient CQD sensor to measure pesticide residues with a detection limit of 5 ppb. The high sensitivity of co-doped CQDs for pesticide detection has been investigated and demonstrated in real-food experiments [98].

Hu et al. developed a novel yellow emission nanoprobe based on N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) by hydrothermal heating of o-phenylenediamine. N-CQDs were used for fluorescent nanoprobes to detect glutathione (GSH) with high sensitivity and selectivity [132]. They observed that mixing N-doped CQDs with glutathione quenched the fluorescence due to static interaction. Meanwhile, fluorescent nanoprobes have been effectively used to detect GSH accurately in fruit and vegetable samples. The detection limits were as low as 0.059 μM and 5.54 μM, respectively. Wang et al. introduced a fast fluorescent switchable approach for highly sensitive and selective determination of tertiary butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) by adopting the competitive interaction between the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect of CQDs/Fe(III) ions and the complexation reaction of TBHQ/Fe(III) ions [133]. The sensing principle of this fluorescent sensor is illustrated in Fig. 6 a. The emission of fluorescence or photons follows the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) from the molecule to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) excitation of electrons. Excited electrons on the CD surface return to the ground state responsible for fluorescence emission. When the Fe(III) ion is exposed, the excited electron enters the Fe(III) ion's unfilled orbital. This photoinduced electron transfer provides a mechanism for non-radiative inactivation of the excited state, leading to fluorescence quenching. Then, the strong complexation reaction between TBHQ and Fe(III) ions in the presence of TBHQ restored the fluorescence of the CD-Fe(III) ion system. With a detection limit as low as 0.01 μg/mL, this fluorescence switchable detection platform makes it applicable for trace detection of TBHQ in complex food matrices.

Electrochemical sensors have also been very efficient in determining hazardous substances and drugs in food samples. For example, Muthusankar et al. reported a simple electrochemical approach for the sensitive detection of aspirin (ASA), a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agent, in fruits using N-doped CQD (N-CQD) decorated Cu2O [134]. The working electrode was fabricated by depositing N-CQD and Cu2O on a glassy carbon electrode (N-CQD/GCE), which greatly enhanced the electrochemical oxidation of ASA with a higher current response. They found that the N-CQDS provides a low detection limit of 0.002 μM with an excellent linear relationship in the range of 1–907 μM and a sensitivity of around 21.87 μA/μM.cm2. Supchocksoonthorn et al. reported hydrothermally assisted iota-carrageenan-derived CQDs as the electrode materials in the electrochemical probes to detect capsaicin. They suggested that the CQDs facilitate the electron transfer between electrodes and capsaicin. They reported an ultra-low detection limit of 5.4 nM to detect capsaicin and a wide linear range of 0.05–500 μM [135].

Yu et al. synthesized a novel citric acid-based red emission fluorescent CQDs (r-CDs) sensor for the dual-mode sensing of nitrite in meat samples, which was capable of both accurate fluorometric detection and convenient colorimetric analysis [136]. In the presence of NO2 −, the interaction between the amino group of r-CDs and NO2 − leads to the form of a diazotized substance, resulting in a colorimetric and fluorescent change in color. Amino groups on the surface of r-CDs interacted with the nitrite ions and produced diazotized substance, resulting in the colorimetric change of the r-CDs solution from rose red to light purple. The red fluorescence was gradually quenched (Fig. 6b). Simultaneously, after adding NO2 − to the UV absorption spectrum, a new characteristic peak appeared around 386 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 602 nm decreases. The fluorescence and colorimetric detection limit of r-CDs against NO2 − was found to be 0.149 μM and 0.193 μM, respectively.

Heavy metals have always been a concern due to their high toxicity to humans, as they can enter the food chain and pose risks to the environment and human health [137]. Therefore, there is a growing interest in detecting heavy metals in food and various environments and measuring their contamination levels. Recent research on quantum dots has made them an excellent choice for heavy metal detection. CQDs are of interest among these QDs due to their high biological activity, biocompatibility, and safety. When a toxic compound or heavy metal reacts with the CQD or its complex, the fluorescence is turned off, and the luminescence intensity decreases in proportion to the concentration of the target compound. CQD-based probes as fluorescent sensors are applied successfully in food analysis. A decrease in CQD emission intensity was observed with increasing metal ion concentration due to the high affinity of CQDs for metal ions [130].

As a fluorescent probe, CQD was used for the sensitive detection of Hg2+, a decrease in CQD emission intensity was observed with increasing Hg2+ ion concentration, and the detection limit reached 0.014 μmol/L [119]. Athika et al. used a CQDs sensor for the actual detection of Cr6+ ions. The CQDs sensor had a detection limit of 14 μM for Cr6+ ions and showed a linear detection ability according to the concentration of Cr6+ ions [120]. Huang et al. found that the Fe3+ addition to the CQDs solution showed fluorescence quenching of CQDs. They also found that CQDs exhibit a synergistic effect of binding capacity due to surface functional groups and play an essential role in CQD-mediated fluorescence ion sensing applications [121]. Lv et al. reported an easy pyrolytic synthesis method for developing highly crystalline N-CQDs using chitosan and tartaric acid as precursors by doping heteroatoms [138]. Due to the chelation between the surface functional groups of CQDs and Fe3+, the CQDs’ fluorescence could be efficiently quenched by Fe3+ ions. However, the fluorescence of CQDs was restored when l-ascorbic acid (AA) was added. The introduction of AA reduces the Fe3+ ions available to detect AA. As a result, a meager detection limit (0.02 M) was obtained. Therefore, AA in vegetable and fruit juices can also be effectively detected based on this mechanism.

One of the innovative applications of CQD sensors is using the unique fluorescence properties of CQD sensors to detect harmful microorganisms and toxins that contaminate food. The fluorescence on-off-on detection method facilitates detection and high-sensitivity analysis [67]. CQD sensors have stable and robust luminescence properties and are considered suitable probe materials for this purpose. For instance, CQDs have been used for detecting Salmonella Typhimurium, aflatoxin B1, and Staphylococcal enterotoxin B [129]. Carbon dot-aptamer complexes were assembled with 76% quantum yield for specific detection of Salmonella Typhimurium by fluorescence mechanism. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs achieved a detection limit of 50 CFU/mL and displayed a linear relationship with bacteria in the ranges of 103–105 CFU/mL. Also, the practical detection of Salmonella Typhimurium using CQDs has been validated in egg samples [97].

Interestingly, the practical application of CQDs in detecting toxins secreted by pathogens has been confirmed. In this regard, Wang et al. developed graphene quantum dots for sensing high-sensitivity fluorescent ochratoxin A [112], and Tang et al. developed enzyme-nanosilica-doped carbon nanotubes for specific detection of Staphylococcal enterotoxin B in food [113]. Besides, Wang et al. constructed new fluorescent CQDs with high sensitivity detection toward aflatoxin B1. Positively charged nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCQDs) were assembled on aptamer/gold nanoparticles. The assay solution showed a linear response to aflatoxin B1 in 5 pg/mL to 2.00 ng/mL with a detection limit of 5 pg/mL (16 pM) [96]. Hu et al. (2021) synthesized CQDs using a green one-step route for the selective and sensitive assay of E. coli O157: H7. The synthesized CQDs showed strong green fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, which played an essential role as a fluorescent probe for E. coli detection in milk samples [139].

CQDs have also been studied to improve their functions as biosensors by changing fluorescence signals in response to interaction with specific molecules or environmental conditions. In particular, to expand sensing applications, some recent studies have aimed to devise methods of using CQDs to represent markers, including changes in pH, gas generation, or water activity [140]. This ability to change the fluorescence of CQDs by interacting with specific substances could be exploited to develop fluorescent nanosensors to detect changes in pH in various environments, including food [67]. Some recent studies have shown the ability of CQDs for this purpose. For example, fluorescein isothiocyanate was conjugated with CQDs modified with polyethyleneimine for pH sensing in yogurt samples [123]. Yang et al. [111] used N-doped CQDs in N, N-dimethylformamide to sense pH. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs decreased linearly when the pH changed from 1.5 to 7.5. These early findings provided an encouraging opportunity to explore the future development and application of CQD sensors for pH changes in the environment.

Further, Kilic et al. fabricated multifunction anthocyanins doped fish gelatin (FG) film using CQDs as a potential crosslinker. Based on the ammonia sensitivity, the CQDs incorporated FG-films were tested for colorimetric food spoilage monitoring using a smartphone application (Fig. 7 a–g) [141]. The color difference of CQDs added FG-films was found to correlate well with total volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N) release and microbial growth in skinless chicken breast samples, signifying its potential for applications in real-time monitoring of food spoilage. Based on the colorimetric response obtained from the CQDs added FG-films, a smartphone application (SmartFood) proficient in image processing was designed for quantitative analysis of food spoilage. The developed analyzing tool in the SmartFood, monitors the spoilage level and conveys to the user about the actual freshness quality of the packaged food.

In another report, Zhang et al. prepared pH-responsive red emission CQDs (R-CDs) using urea and citric acid as the primary carbon precursor [108]. The synthesized R-CDs were used for the visual detection of biogenic amines (BAs) and alkaline organic amines released from rotten shrimp during storage. For this, pH-responsive R-CDs treated filter paper was placed inside the fresh shrimp container and stored at room temperature and 4 °C for 1–3 days. After the third day of packaging, the red fluorescence of the R-CDs-based filter paper had become particularly noticeable under UV light. However, for the same period, no significant change was observed in the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs-based filter paper for the shrimp stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for three days. At room temperature, the decay of the shrimp caused the release of BAs, which left the R-CDs in an alkaline environment, resulting in the enhanced emission of red light.

Further, a symmetrical R-CDs treated filter paper was immersed into the solution of pH 2.5, which displayed a faint red emission. At the same time, it was observed that the filter paper treated with the pH 12 solution emitted red light under UV (365 nm). Based on this experiment, they concluded that pH-responsive R-CDS could be employed as ‘‘anti-counterfeiting ink’’ which has great prospects and importance in information encryption. Recently, Koshy et al. prepared a smart packaging film using a functional indicator made of CQDs, anthocyanin, and starch film. The authors discovered that adding CQDs and anthocyanin enhanced the films' UV barrier, mechanical, and antioxidant activities with pH-responsive color-changing properties. The starch film displayed a significant color change between pink in acidic conditions and yellow in alkaline conditions. When monitoring the freshness of pork samples, alkaline gas generated during storage affects the color of the composite membrane, and this effect was correlated with the increase in TVB-N (27.39 mg/100 g at 48 h) and pH (8) values [142].

ce:section.ce:section-title: A new perspective on using CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, the microplastic problem is recognized as a major disaster for humankind. Accordingly, although there have been many studies on the detection of microplastics over the past decade, the analysis of microplastics has been hampered by several limitations. The distribution of plastics is very heterogeneous and varies depending on the sample type. Above all, there is a problem that objective comparison is difficult because a standard analysis method for microplastics has not yet been established [143].

Various analytical techniques have been used to detect microplastics, including visual methods, thermochemical methods, FTIR spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and fluorescent detection methods [144]. These analytical methods have detection limits based on the sample's particle size, shape, or distribution [144]. Even though these methods, individually or in combination, have been used to detect microplastics in the samples successfully, there are some difficulties in detecting microplastics accurately, particularly in organic samples like food. The presence of possible organic matter with microplastics results in false-positive signals [143]. Above all, it is difficult to compare the results because a standard method for microplastic analysis has not yet been established [143].

By the way, the size and characteristics of CQD can be adjusted depending on the raw material, manufacturing method, and conditions. In particular, the properties of CQDs can be modulated through functionalization and surface modification as functional materials [37]. Functionalized CQDs help to interact with biological surfaces, providing tremendous potential for sensing applications. Besides, CQD's low toxicity and excellent antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant activity make it a promising material for direct contact food packaging and biological system applications [37,70].

The fluorescence of CQDs differs from the fluorescence of currently used fluorophores in the following ways: (i) CQDs emit a fluorescent signal in response to incident excitation radiation of a specific wavelength, usually in the UV region of the spectrum. (ii) CQD can be used to obtain similar fluorescence signals that will improve the reproducibility of detection. (iii) The fluorescence of CQDs can be tuned by changing the size and core and surface functionalization. (iv) Since functionalization will be responsible for the interaction, functionalized CQDs can be engineered to engineer the core and surface of CQDs in combination with specific organic compounds or analyte-sensing elements. Therefore, adequately functionalized CQDs are expected to have a high potential for use as fluorescent tags for microplastic detection in environmental/food samples. The successful application of CQDs to detect heavy metals or toxic compounds and changes in pH values has already been reported. Thus, CQDs can prove to be potentially cost-effective, high-precision, and target-specific fluorescent tags for the detection of microplastics in environmental and food samples.

Although the benefits and applications of CQDs and their derivatives have promising prospects, their applications in food technology are relatively new compared to their applications in other areas, such as bioimaging, photonic devices, pharmaceuticals, and drug delivery. However, ongoing research into discoveries could completely revolutionize food systems through new storage technologies, innovative packaging, and modifications to essential food functions. In recent years, the food industry has made great strides in food safety issues by inventing new functional substances, pesticides, and pathogen detection through indicators and biosensors and the controlled release of bioactive compounds and food transport. In addition, intelligent food packaging incorporating CQD-based sensors could be a reliable, accurate, and fast technology that can provide consumers with real-time information about the quality of packaged food. However, in addition to explaining the benefits of CQD for the food industry, the application of intelligent food packaging for commercial purposes should focus on various factors such as health concerns, product cost, and the impact of packaging materials.

Additionally, there are several considerations regarding applying nanomaterial-based sensors to packaged foods. First, nanosensors' detection specificity, sensitivity, and limitations should be considered. Nevertheless, the excellent PL properties of CQDs show the potential to reveal the integrity of products in a short time of less than 1 min, so their application in food safety and quality is expected. However, research on this is still in its infancy, so continuous research is needed to apply the CQD-based sensing system to commercial-scale food technology.

The following points should be considered to overcome these limitations and develop and realize stable commercialization of a CQD-based sensing platform.
(i)
The fabricated sensor's structure and size must maintain the food's integrity over time and accurately indicate the quality of the packaged food. The particle size of nanomaterials is an essential parameter for food packaging and must be verified as safe.
(ii)
Before applying innovative food sensing technology, a good review of the impact of nanomaterial-based sensors on human health and socioeconomic problems should be made.
(iii)
The safety of multifunctional and multi-reactive materials used in nanosensors must be secured.
(iv)
It is necessary to secure safe raw materials and develop green additives to produce CQD-based derivatives that can meet the desired properties. To this end, some sophisticated instruments and complex nanotechnology required to construct nanocomposites remain significant challenges.
v)
The sensor must remain stable over time within its shelf life, be sensitive to the analyte of interest, and have no adverse effects, especially when combined with packaged foods.
In addition, the final cost of an intelligent sensing system must be cost-effective for the end consumer.

Intelligent sensing technology is a fast-growing field in the food packaging industry that will soon revolutionize food safety. Developing and integrating CQD-based detection systems for packaged food and smart packaging innovation is critical to promoting food security. This advanced food security platform supports communication, documentation, analysis, identification, and management of the food supply chain. Due to their unique PL properties, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, biocompatibility, and eco-friendliness, CQDs with diverse activities and high specific surface area are promising and attractive materials that can be used as alternatives to conventional detection and analysis methods. The developed CQD-based detection probe can be used in intelligent barcodes or packaging encapsulated or scanned into food packaging to convey food quality and safety information.

However, CQD and its derivatives in food packaging applications are at the infant stage. Much further research is needed to generate valuable data for the security prospects of intelligent sensing using CQDs in food technology. The number of ecological CQD-based sensing probes will grow exponentially with the current trend toward more effective nanomaterials and more accurate analytes. Ongoing research will open up new possibilities for the food industry to construct CQD-based packaging materials to preserve healthy and safe food. We anticipate that the current progress of CQD will overcome the limitations of current food safety issues and become an innovative tool for the future development of intelligent sensing and packaged food.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT, grant number: 2022R1A2B5B02001422).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A vision on the “foodture” role of dietary exposure sciences in the interplay between food safety and nutrition
Trends Food Sci. Technol.
